coredata.dc:title: Nanoarchitecture of variable sized graphene nanosheets incorporated into three-dimensional graphene network for dye sensitized solar cells
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Quanhong
Huang, Lei
Wang, Jinzhong
Ma, Zhoujing
Li, Panpan
Yan, Yuan
Zhu, Jianxiao
Xu, Shuhua
Shen, Leo
Chen, Qi
Yu, Qingjiang
Shi, Wangzhou
coredata.dc:description: A novel three-dimensional (3D) nanoarchitecture consisting of hybrid graphene nanosheets (GNs)/graphene foam (GF) was fabricated on the FTO conducting substrate as a high efficient counter electrode (CE) for dye sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). The GNs with various sized such as large-sized heat-reduced graphene nanosheets (H-GNs) and small-sized laser-reduced graphene quantum dots (L-GQDs) were synthesized and used as catalytic materials incorporated into a 3D GF network, respectively. In this design, the aggregations and restacking of GNs were efficiently reduced, which is beneficial for increasing the amount of the active defective sites at the edges of graphene to the electrolyte solution. Especially, L-GQDs with smaller dimension less than 100nm have more active defective sites at edges, providing superiority over the large-sized H-GNs in terms of electrocatalytic activity. Meanwhile, the GF network with high conductivity provides fast electron transport channels for charge injection between the GNs and FTO. The DSSC with this hybrid CE exhibited energy conversion efficiency (η) of 7.70% with an open circuit voltage (V OC), short circuit photocurrent density (J SC) and fill factor (FF) of 760mV, 15.21mAcm−2, and 72.0%, respectively, which is comparable to that of the conventional Pt CE (7.68%).

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Pt, hybrid CEs, and fabrication of DSSCs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the fabrication processes of hybrid graphene-based CE for DSSCs. (a) the GF was synthesized on a Ni foam template by CVD; (b) the GF was transferred onto a FTO substrate after etching the Ni template by using FeCl3/HCl solution; (c) H-GNs or L-GQDs solution was coated on GF/FTO; (d) the fabrication of DSSC. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) SEM image of the GF structures showing a continuous network of 3D interconnected graphene sheets; (b) typical Raman spectra obtained at various locations on the GF, demonstrating that the foam walls consist of mono- to few-layer graphene; (c and d) SEM images of the hybrid H-GNs/GF and L-GQDs/GF, respectively. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and their corresponding BJH pore size distribution (inset) calculated from the desorption branches of the GF (a), hybrid H-GNs/GF (b) and L-GQDs/GF (c). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Cyclic voltammetry of CEs with various CEs (Pt, GF, H-GNs, H-GNs/GF and L-GQDs/GF) obtained at scan rate of 50mVs−1 in acetonitrile electrolyte solution containing 5mM LiI, 0.5mM I2 and 0.1M LiClO4. The counter electrode was a Pt wire and the reference electrode was Ag/AgCl. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of Pt, GF, H-GNs, optimized hybrid H-GNs/GF and L-GQDs/GF CEs in a symmetric sandwich cell configuration, and the corresponding the equivalent circuit diagram for fitting the EIS (R S is the series resistance, R CT is the charge transfer resistance at the CE/electrolyte interface, W pore is the Warburg impedance within CE layers, CPE is constant phase element of one electrode, and W bulk is the Warburg impedance between the two CEs); (b) The magnified EIS of Pt, H-GNs/GF and L-GQDs/GF CEs from (a). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photocurrent–voltage characteristics of DSSCs with various CEs (Pt, GF, H-GNs, H-GNs/GF and L-GQDs/GF) measured under AM 1.5 simulated solar illumination. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Photovoltaic parameters of DSSCs fabricated with various CEs. The values R CT were deduced from Fig. 5.

head.ce:title: Nanoarchitecture of variable sized graphene nanosheets incorporated into three-dimensional graphene network for dye sensitized solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Joint Lab with Wuhu Token for Graphene Electrical Materials and Application, Department of Physics, Shanghai Normal University, Guilin Road 100, Shanghai 200234, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Joint Lab with Wuhu Token for Graphene Electrical Materials and Application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Opto-electric Information Materials and Quantum Devices, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, 150001 Harbin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Opto-electric Information Materials and Quantum Devices
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel three-dimensional (3D) nanoarchitecture consisting of hybrid graphene nanosheets (GNs)/graphene foam (GF) was fabricated on the FTO conducting substrate as a high efficient counter electrode (CE) for dye sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). The GNs with various sized such as large-sized heat-reduced graphene nanosheets (H-GNs) and small-sized laser-reduced graphene quantum dots (L-GQDs) were synthesized and used as catalytic materials incorporated into a 3D GF network, respectively. In this design, the aggregations and restacking of GNs were efficiently reduced, which is beneficial for increasing the amount of the active defective sites at the edges of graphene to the electrolyte solution. Especially, L-GQDs with smaller dimension less than 100nm have more active defective sites at edges, providing superiority over the large-sized H-GNs in terms of electrocatalytic activity. Meanwhile, the GF network with high conductivity provides fast electron transport channels for charge injection between the GNs and FTO. The DSSC with this hybrid CE exhibited energy conversion efficiency (η) of 7.70% with an open circuit voltage (V OC), short circuit photocurrent density (J SC) and fill factor (FF) of 760mV, 15.21mAcm−2, and 72.0%, respectively, which is comparable to that of the conventional Pt CE (7.68%).

ce:section.ce:para: Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) have drawn a lot of attentions due to its high conversion efficiency and low fabrication cost [1–7]. A typical DSSC consists of a dye-adsorbed TiO2 photoanode, iodide electrolyte solution and counter electrode (CE). Although the photoanode has the greatest potential to improve the efficiency of the DSSCs, the CE also has an important role in reducing triiodide to iodide [8,9]. To date, Pt has been widely exploited as the typical CE in DSSCs due to its excellent electrocatalytic activity for the reduction of triiodide, high conductivity and outstanding chemical stability [10]. However, the high cost of Pt and its limited reserves in nature have been the major concern for its application as CE in DSSCs. Therefore, it inspires us to explore promising Pt-free electrocatalytic materials with low cost and excellent electrocatalytic activity.

Since Kay and Grätzel firstly reported a graphite/carbon black mixture as a CE due to its low cost and abundance [11], carbon materials such as carbon black, carbon nanoparticles, carbon nanotubes and graphene nanosheets already have been the candidates as the CEs for DSSCs [12–15]. As a rising star in the carbon family, graphene, which is a novel two-dimensional material with a single-layer hexagonal lattice of carbon atoms, is being expected to be a promising material as CE for DSSC due to its excellent electron mobility, high reactivity for triiodide reduction and high electrochemical stability under prolonged potential cycling [1–5]. Since the electrocatalytic activity of graphene for reducing the triiodide often increases with the number of active defective sites [16,17], a perfect graphene sheet may have a low charge-transfer resistance (R CT), but a limited number of active sites for catalyzing the triiodide reduction [1–5,16–18]. According to the high efficiency DSSCs fabricated using the N-doped porous graphene CEs by Dai [19] and Hu et al. [20], the active sites for catalyzing the triiodide reduction in graphene are located at the defective sites and crystal edges of graphene. So far, the high efficiency (η =6.81%) DSSC with GNs as a CE has been achieved by Huang et al. [12] . However, due to the strong π–π interaction between the large basal planes, the main barrier hindering the development of DSSCs with GNs as CE is that GNs tend to easily aggregate, which greatly reduces the amount of active defective sites at the edges of graphene for I3 −/I− electrocatalysis and weakens the electrical properties of graphene [21–24]. Therefore, how to reduce the aggregations and increase the active defective sites for balancing its electrical conductivity and the electrocatalytic activity may be the promising research interest for GNs-based DSSCs.

Although GNs have shown great promise as CEs for DSSCs, more attentions have been focused on the hybrid nanostructure CEs with GNs to enhance their electrocatalytic activity. Recently, high efficient graphene/NiO and graphene/Pt nanohybrids as CEs were successfully synthesized by Choi et al. [1,2] . The electrochemical impedance measurement of the DSSCs revealed that the R CT of the nanohybrid CE was close to that of a Pt-sputtered CE. Additionally, Li et al. demonstrated a high energy conversion efficiency (η =5.27%) DSSC with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) doping into the polypyrrole (PPy) film as an efficient hybrid CE [25] . In this hybrid structure, small-sized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) proved that edge-bound graphene nanosheets with nanometer size have more active defective sites at the edges, which exhibit an enhanced electrocatalytic activity and higher charge transfer rate. Besides, the incorporation of GQDs results in a porous CE, which increase electrocatalytic centers and enhance reactant diffusion for better electrode kinetics. In addition, Jang et al. demonstrated a nanostructure of hybrid Pt/graphene composite CE synthesized via the self-assembly of polyelectrolyte, graphene and H2PtCl6 [26]. The DSSC with this hybrid nanostructure CE achieved η of 8.91%, comparable to that (8.85%) of the DSSC with the state-of-the-art Pt-based CE. Importantly, Pt nanoparticles can be taken into account as zero-dimensional nanospacers between the graphene layers, which efficiently prevent the restacking of graphene layers and increase the active defective sites caused by incorporating Pt nanoparticles into the GNs. Furthermore, a three-dimensional (3D) hybrid carbon framework CE rebuilt by incorporating carbon nanotubes (CNTs) as one-dimensional nanospacers into the layers of GNs was reported by Li-Hsueh Chang et al. [27]. This hybrid nanoarchitecture reduces the aggregations of the GNs, resulting in a low charge transfer resistance (R CT) 2.94Ωcm2. These facts, coupled with the active defective sites at the edges of GNs, should inspire researchers to fabricate novel 3D nanoarchitecture as high efficient CEs for DSSCs. As a proof of concept, Dai et al. prepared the high efficiency DSSCs with the N-doped 3D graphene foam CE [19]. In this heteroatom doped 3D carbon network, the graphitic basal plan has a minimized change in the conjugated structure, which ensures the CE with enhanced electrical conductivity and electrocatalytic activity. Recently, 3D hybrid Ag/GNs as highly efficient CE for DSSCs is described in our group [28], highlighting the Ag nanoparticles as zero-dimensional nanospacers inserting into GNs to lift the interspacing layer between individual GNs. Compared to their pure GNs, hybrid Ag/GNs without aggregations have a significant improvement in their electrochemical properties such as high current density, narrow peak-to-peak separation (E pp) and low R CT. The enhancement of electrochemical performance can be attributed to the increased electrode conductivity, an extended interlayer distance and the reduction of the restacking of GNs due to the insertion of Ag nanoparticles into GNs.

In 2012, Cheng and co-workers synthesized graphene foam (GF) using porous Ni foam as a template by chemical vapor deposition (CVD), followed by etching away the Ni skeleton [29,30]. The GF consists of an interconnected flexible network of graphene with a more porous structure and extremely high conductivity (10s/cm), which is beneficial to provide the fast transport channels for charge carriers. Therefore, in the present work, in order to make full use of the active defective sites at the edges of GNs and unique properties of the newly discovered GF, a novel 3D hybrid GNs/GF nanoarchitecture as an efficient CE for DSSC was proposed. With CVD grown GF as a 3D network with high specific surface area, various sized GNs such as large-sized heat-reduced graphene nanosheets (H-GNs) and small-sized laser-reduced graphene quantum dots (L-GQDs) were synthesized and used as the catalytic materials incorporated into a 3D GF network. In this novel 3D hybrid CE, the GNs were isolated from each other to prevent the aggregations and restacking of GNs, which is beneficial to expose the active defective sites of GNs to electrolyte solution. The effect caused by the size of GNs was investigated for optimizing the performance of 3D hybrid graphene-based CEs. The fabricated DSSC with this 3D hybrid GNs/GF CE showed energy conversion efficiency as high as 7.70%, a value which is higher than that of DSSCs with a Pt CE (7.68%).

Similar to that previously reported [29,30], 3D GF was synthesized by CVD using Ni foam as a template. The Ni foam placed into a quartz tube was heated to 880°C at a heating rate of 10°C/min and maintained for 30min under a pressure of 10Torr with a mixed gas flow of H2/N2 (H2:N2 =10sccm:50sccm) to clean the surface of nickel foam. Subsequently, the CVD growth of GF was triggered by the addition of C2H2 (8sccm) along with N2 for 5min. After CVD growth, the graphene-Ni foam is rapidly cooled to the room temperature at a cooling rate (100°C/min) under the H2/N2 flow. After the Ni foam was etched away with FeCl3/HCl solution at room temperature, the 3D GF with an average pristine thickness of 0.2mm was transferred onto a FTO conducting substrate. Then, a few drops of ethanol were dripped onto the GF sheet to wet the GF, and a glass rod was used to press the GF tightly onto the FTO substrate. After drying on air, the GF tightly adhere to the FTO substrate due to the van der Waals forces [6]. Compared with its pristine thickness, after the above compressing and drying process, the thickness of the GF approximately approaches about 40μm.

Graphite powder (2g) was added to cool (2°C) concentrated H2SO4 (150ml). KMnO4 (9g) was added gradually with stirring and cooling so that the temperature of the mixture was maintained below 20°C. Then, the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 7days. Distilled water (250ml) was slowly added to cause an increase in temperature to 98°C and maintained at that temperature for 15min. H2O2 solution (6ml, 30%) was added when the temperature was about 60°C. The sample was separated by centrifugation, and then washed repeatedly with HCl and DI water. The suspension was dried in a vacuum oven at 60°C for 24h to obtain graphene oxide (GO). The obtained powder of GO was heat-reduced to H-GNs at 700°C in a quartz boat under H2-containing atmosphere for 0.5h in a horizontal furnace. Then, the H-GNs solution was prepared with different concentration. A homogeneous GO aqueous suspension (0.5mg/ml) was achieved by dispersing purified GO in DI water with the aid of ultrasonication for 1h. Then, ammonia was added into the above GO suspension to present in solution at pH 9. Afterward, 15ml yellow–brown GO suspension was loaded into a quartz tube for the pulsed laser reduction, as described in our work [24]. In this work, the laser energy and repetition rate was 250mJ and 3Hz, respectively. After the pulsed laser irradiation for 1h, the GO sheets were reduced into L-GQDs with the lateral dimension less than 100nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Pt, hybrid CEs, and fabrication of DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorine-doped tin oxide coated glass (FTO) was firstly cleaned in a detergent solution using an ultrasonic bath for 15min, and then rinsed with ethanol and DI water, followed by a 30min treatment in a UVO-Cleaner (model 256-220, Jelight Co., Inc.). The state-of-the-art Pt CE was prepared by the thermal reduction method, in which a Pt precursor (10mM H2PtCl6·H2O) was drop-casted on the FTO glass, followed by thermal treatment at 450°C for 20min. As shown in Fig. 1 , the H-GNs and L-GQDs were dispersed in 2-propanol by sonication for 5min and 15min respectively, and then the solution was left overnight to separate big particles by sedimentation. The volume of H-GNs and L-GQDs solutions were adjusted by varying the concentration of the used dispersion (0.1, 0.5, 1.0 and 2mg/ml). The optimized dispersions containing about 1.0mg/ml of H-GNs and 1.5mg/ml of L-GQDs were stable for several days without marked sedimentation, respectively. The stock graphene dispersion was sometimes diluted to concentrations 0.1mg/L and 0.5mg/ml. Subsequently, the dispersion was spin-coated on the GF/FTO substrate with the rate of 1000rpm for 20s. Finally, the hybrid H-GNs/GF and L-GQDs/GF were dried in a vacuum at 120°C overnight. In order to prepare the photoanodes for the DSSC, a layer of TiO2 paste was coated on FTO conducting substrate to achieve a thickness about 14μm. Then, the electrodes were sintered at 500°C for 30min in air. The fabricated TiO2 photoanodes have more porous structures, which is beneficial to achieve high conversion efficiency for DSSCs. Finally, the TiO2 photoanodes were immersed in 0.5mM N719 dye solution in a mixture of acetonitrile and tert-butyl alcohol (volume ratio: 1:1) at room temperature for 24h to complete the sensitizer uptake, as described in our previous reports [31,32]. The dye-sensitized TiO2 photoanodes were subsequently washed with acetonitrile and further sandwiched with the CEs. The intervening space in the sandwich-type cell was filled with a drop of the electrolyte solution (0.60M 1-methyl-3-propylimidazolium iodide (BMII), 0.03M I2, 0.10M guanidinium thiocyanate and 0.50M 4-tert-butylpyridine in the mixture of acetonitrile and valeronitrile (volume ratio: 85:15).

The morphology of the hybrid CEs was examined by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JSM-7000F, Japan) and transmittance electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL-1400). Raman analysis (Jobin Yvon LabRam-010, with laser excitation wavelength of 632.8nm and a spot size of ∼1μm2) was performed to measure the bonding characteristics, the defect and quality of the CVD grown GF. The BET surface area and the pore size distribution of the prepared samples were analyzed using micromeritics Tristar II. Elemental composition analysis was carried out using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI 5000 Versa Probe). The electrochemical responses (CV and EIS) were investigated by an electrochemical station (CH Instruments, CHI660B). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were carried out at a scan rate of 50mVs−1 in a three-electrode system consisting of Pt wire as a CE, Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode and the newly prepared various electrodes as the working electrodes in the potential window of −0.2 to 0.8V in acetonitrile solution containing 5mM LiI, 0.5mM I2 and 0.1M LiClO4. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of various CEs were measured with a frequency range from 100kHz to 0.1Hz in a symmetric sandwich cell configuration. The photocurrent–voltage characteristics of DSSCs were obtained under AM 1.5, 100mWcm−2 illumination using a solar light simulator (Sun 2000).

The fabrication processes of the 3D hybrid graphene-based CEs for DSSCs are shown in Fig. 1: (a) the GF was synthesized on a Ni foam template by CVD; (b) the GF was transferred onto a FTO conducting substrate after etching the Ni template by using FeCl3/HCl solution; (c) large-sized H-GNs or small-sized L-GQDs were coated on the GF/FTO; (d) the fabrication of DSSCs.

Fig. 2 a shows a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of 3D GF network after etching the Ni template. It is clearly observed that the GF copies and inherits the interconnected 3D scaffold network of the nickel foam template. Meanwhile, all the graphene sheets in the GF are well separated and directly connect to each other without breaks, indicating a continuous 3D GF network with high conductivity (10s/cm), which is close to that reported by Cheng et al. [29,30]. As shown in Fig. 2b, typical Raman spectra exhibit two distinct peaks at 1580cm−1 (G-band) and 2660cm−1 (2D-band), demonstrating that the 3D GF network contains mono- to few-layer graphene [31]. Whereas, the D-band at 1350cm−1 associated with the disordered defect in graphene is strongly suppressed [32,33], indicating that the GF network has a high quality and is able to provide the fast transport channels for charge carriers. TEM images together with the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) of the synthesized large-size H-GNs shown in Fig. S1 (supporting information) tell that the large-sized H-GNs have high quality after heat reduction of GO. The SEM image of 3D hybrid H-GNs/GF nanoarchitecture CE is displayed in Fig. 2c. It is clearly observed that the individual H-GNs appear to be well dispersed into the network surface of the GF and some of H-GNs without aggregations are closely attaching into the 3D GF network. Compared with the fresh H-GNs consisting of randomly aggregations and crumpled sheets due to the π–π interaction between the large basal graphene sheets, as shown in Fig. S2c (supporting information) [21], the restacking of graphene sheets was efficiently reduced after incorporating the H-GNs into 3D GF network, reducing the loss of their active sites for I3 −/I− redox reaction [34,35]. Besides, the porous diameter of the GF became smaller after the incorporation of the H-GNs, as shown in Fig. 2c, providing a stable reservoir for the electrolyte [6]. Based on this 3D hybrid GNs/GF nanoarchitecture (Fig. 2c), the distribution characteristic of H-GNs and the high conductivity of GF should improve the interface interaction between the H-GNs/GF CE and the I3 −/I− electrolyte solution, and offers a unique opportunity for its application in DSSC. Taking into consideration that the defects located at edges of GNs are responsible for the electrocatalytic performance of CE in DSSCs [4,16,35,36], small-sized L-GQDs as catalytic materials were prepared by pulsed laser irradiation to be spin-coated on the GF/FTO [24]. As shown in Fig. S3 (supporting information), the SEM, TEM images and SAED of small-sized L-GQDs together demonstrated that the L-GQDs with an average dimension less than 100nm has high crystal quality, similar to the GQDs and sub-micrometer-size graphite synthesized by Berry [37] and Rhee [38], respectively. Just as displayed in Fig. 2d, the silk-like small-sized L-GQDs with some wrinkles are attaching onto the GF. According to the results reported by Rhee et al. [38], the increase of active defective sites is concomitant with a proportional increase in the ratio of the edge to basal plane area from large-sized to small-sized GNs. Therefore, by comparison with the large-sized H-GNs, the small-sized L-GQDs have more edges with more active defective sites for the reduction of I3 − [35,39,40]. Since the active defective sites in the GNs play a role in achieving effective catalytic activities in DSSCs, the small-sized L-GQDs as catalytic materials are of much importance to improve the performance of the graphene-based CEs.

To further investigate the 3D nanoarchitecture of the as-prepared hybrid CEs, the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms at 77K and the corresponding pore size distribution are measured. As displayed in Fig. 3 a, the adsorption–desorption curve of GF network clearly exhibits the prominent Type I, characteristic of microporous materials, and Type IV, characteristic of mesoporous materials with a distinct hysteresis loop of H2 in the P/P 0 range of 0.4–1.0, implying the presence of relatively large macropores and mesopores in the GF network [41,42]. It exhibits micro- (<2nm), meso- (2–50nm), and macroporous (>50nm) structure with a rather high proportion of micropores and mesopores, which could be caused by the slit-shape pores during the transferring process. However, the porous structure of the hybrid H-GNs/GF composite shows the Type I (Fig. 3b), characteristic of microporous materials after incorporating the H-GNs into the macropores of GF, which is consistent with the SEM (Fig. 2c). Additionally, the surface area of the hybrid H-GNs/GF approaches to 330m2 g−1, which is larger than that (166m2 g−1) of the individual GF. Besides, the mesopore size of the hybrid H-GNs/GF calculated by the BJH method ranges from 3 to 10nm with a narrow distribution. As expected, although the macropores still exist in Fig. 3c, the hybrid L-GQDs/GF composite shows the highest proportion of micropores and mesopores due to the layering of smaller L-GQDs on GF and the surface area of the hybrid L-GQDs/GF reach 422m2 g−1, which is beneficial to provide effective channels for the transition of I3 −/I− ions in the electrolyte. Importantly, the large surface area of L-GQDs/GF will endow a high electrochemical catalytic activity to the reduction of the I3 − ions into I− ions that occurs at the interface between L-GQDs surface and the electrolyte.

For clearly exhibiting the electrochemical activity of these novel 3D hybrid CEs, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) were carried out to analyze the electrocatalytic activities of the CEs. The synthesized CEs were placed in an acetonitrile solution containing 5mM LiI, 0.5mM I2 and 0.1M LiClO4. The active area for all the CEs is ∼1cm2. As shown in Fig. 4 , two pairs of oxidation/reduction peaks, Aox/Ared and Box/Bred, correspond to the reactions in Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively [25,27], are observed in the CV curves for the Pt, large-size H-GNs, H-GNs/GF and L-GQDs/GF electrodes, whereas no obvious oxidation/reduction peaks could be observed for the GF electrode.
(1)
3
I
2
+ 2
e
-
→ 2
I
3
-
(2)
I
3
-
+ 2
e
-
→ 3
I
-

In the CVs, the cathodic current density peaks assign to the reduction of I3 − ions through interaction with the CEs. It has been recognized that the cathodic current density represents the electrocatalytic activity of a CE toward reduction of I3 − ions in DSSCs. As shown in Fig. 4, the 3D GF CE displays a low cathodic current density, which implies poor catalytic activity toward the reduction of I3 − ions. However, the 3D hybrid H-GNs/GF CE exhibits a higher cathodic current density than the H-GNs and GF electrode, respectively. From the SEM in Fig. 2c, this could be ascribed to the exposing edges of large-size H-GNs without aggregations in this 3D hybrid nanoarchitecture, which efficiently increases their active defective sites interacting with the electrolyte solution [1,2,4,16,36]. Interestingly, in comparison with the hybrid H-GNs/GF and Pt CE, the hybrid L-GQDs/GF CE has the highest cathodic current density. Additionally, the peak-to-peak separation (E pp) of the left pairs in CV curves can also elucidate redox reaction kinetics at the CEs. The H-GNs CE shows a much wider E pp (450±4mV) than that (370±3mV) of Pt CE, indicating H-GNs CE owns a relative slow redox reaction rate. However, the value of E pp (380±3mV) is reduced considerably in the H-GNs/GF CE. Besides, when the small-sized L-GQDs were coated on GF, the smaller value of E pp (310±2mV) was obtained. This fact implies that incorporating GNs into GF can significantly reduce the overpotential for the I3 − reduction, resulting in the enhancement of the reaction kinetics [43,44]. Therefore, the enhanced electrocatalytic activity in these 3D hybrid nanoarchitecture CEs relies on more active defective sites at more edges of GNs. In addition, the reduction peak for the cathodic current density on the hybrid L-GQDs/GF CE is more positive than that of the H-GNs/GF CE, further revealing that the L-GQDs/GF with more reaction active centers has higher electrocatalytic activities toward reduction of I3 − ions [35,38]. Besides, the enhanced electrocatalytic activity of the L-GQDs also could be deduced from the XPS spectra in Fig. S4c (supporting information). By comparison with GO, most of the oxygen-containing functional groups have been removed after the heat reduction and the pulsed laser irradiation. Particularly, the C–O (epoxy and alkoxy) and CO peaks of L-GQDs are accounted for 8% and 7.6% in the C1s spectra area, which are less than that of H-GNs. Just as reported by Chien-Te Hsieh et al. [45], the removal of oxygen functionalities efficiently facilitates the redox kinetics, thus inducing the high current density on the graphene-based CE. Namely, the graphene-based CEs with different oxidation levels have significantly different electrocatalytic activity and conductivity. Therefore, the difference of oxygen-containing functional groups between the H-GNs and L-GQDs suggests that the conductivity and electrocatalytic activity of L-GQDs will better than that of H-GNs [12].

The electrocatalytic performance of the prepared CEs on the reduction of I3 − ions was further studied by using electrochemical impendence spectroscopy (EIS), as shown in Fig. 5 a. As well known, the impedance spectra form two semicircles which represent the two different steps of the reaction in the electrochemical process, namely the charge transfer at the interface of CE/electrolyte solution and the diffusion of I3 −/I− ions [1–6]. The fitting curves of the EIS spectra were displayed in the Fig. S5 (supporting information). It is clearly observed that two semicircles exist in the EIS spectra. The first semicircle corresponds to R CT at the interface of CE/electrolyte solution, which changes inversely with catalytic ability of CEs for the reduction of I3 − to I−, while the semicircle in lower frequency region is attributed to Nernst diffusion process [27]. The plots were fitted with an equivalent circuit, as shown in the inset of Fig. 5a, consisting of the R CT in series with parallel with a constant phase element (CPE), both of which are in series with the Warburg impedance within electrode pores (W pore) and sheet resistance (R S). The magnified EIS spectra of symmetric cells with H-GNs/GF, L-GQDs/GF and Pt CE are displayed in the Fig. 4b. As the R S due to the sheet resistance of FTO substrate contribute to the internal series resistance of the cell and the value of the CEs are close to each other, the value of R CT will play a key role in the performance of the DSSCs which were fabricated with same anodes and electrolyte but different CEs. As shown in the Table 1 , the catalytic efficiency of CEs was evaluated by R CT, which shows 8.7, 38, 1.30, 1.20 and 1.18Ωcm2 deduced for H-GNs, GF, H-GNs/GF, L-GQDs/GF and Pt electrodes, respectively. Although the value of R CT with less than 3.3% perturbation occur on all the CEs, the R CT still demonstrates the electrocatalytic activity. As mentioned before in the CV (Fig. 4c and d) and SEM (Fig. 2), the incorporation of GNs into the GF electrode leads to an obvious improvement in the electrocatalytic activity due to providing a more active sites for the reduction of I3 −. In addition, compared with that of H-GNs, the values of R CT for both hybrid H-GNs/GF and L-GQDs/GF are seven times less than that of GF CE, and comparable to that of Pt CE. These findings imply that hybrid H-GNs/GF and L-GQDs/GF have more active sites for the reduction of I3 − than that of individual GNs. As shown in Table S1, the value of the constant phase element (CPE=(CPE-T)−1(jω)−(CPE-P), in which j 2 =−1, ω =frequency, CPE-T and CPE-P are frequency-independent parameters of the CPE) of the CE evidences that the active surface area of the L-GQDs/GF CE is larger than that of the thermal reduced Pt electrode and H-GNs/GF CE. Indeed, the value of CPE-T of 3.02×10−3 Fcm−2 for the hybrid L-GQDs/GF is much greater than the 1.12×10−4 Fcm−2 of the thermal reduced Pt electrode and even greater than 2.16×10−3 Fcm−2 of the H-GNs/GF electrode. A larger CPE-T means an increase in the active surface area, and a decrease of CPE-P means an increase in the porosity [1,2]. We found that CPE-P is 0.12 for the hybrid L-GQDs/GF CE and 0.20 for the H-GNs/GF CE, which are much smaller than that of the thermal reduced Pt electrode, viz. 0.90.

DSSCs were fabricated to demonstrate the performance of optimized hybrid CEs in a practical application. Fig. 6 shows the photocurrent–voltage characteristics of DSSCs with Pt, GF, H-GNs, H-GNs/GF and L-GQDs/GF CEs under AM 1.5, 100mWcm−2 illumination using a solar light simulator (Sun 2000), respectively. The derived photovoltaic parameters such as the open-circuit voltage (V OC), the short-circuit current density (J SC), the fill factor (FF) and the conversion efficiency (η) are summarized in Table 1. It is clearly observed that the GF as the CE yields a low conversion efficiency of 0.68%, consistent with the CV and EIS results. Whereas, the DSSC with the 3D hybrid H-GNs/GF CE exhibits a V OC of 0.74 V, J SC of 13.52mAcm−2, FF of 0.70, and η of 7.10%, which is close to that of the cell with Pt CE (7.68%) and higher than that (6.81%) of the DSSC with graphene-based CE reported by Huang et al. [12]. Furthermore, the small-sized L-GQDs with more active defective sites at edges provide the superiority over the H-GNs in terms of electrocatalytic activity, just as shown in Figs. 4 and 5. The DSSC with the small-sized L-GQDs/GF as hybrid CE exhibits the V OC of 0.75V, FF of 0.71 and η of 7.70%, which is comparable to that (7.68%) of the DSSC with Pt CE. To study the durability and stability of the DSSCs with the hybrid H-GNs/GF CE and L-GQDs/GF CE, the DSSCs were subjected to light soaking with full solar intensity (100mW/cm2). As shown in Table S2 (supporting information), after 1000h of light soaking, all the DSSCs exhibit excellent stability. Particularly, the DSSC with the hybrid L-GQDs/GF CE only shows a drop of less than 6% in efficiency during 1000h aging. Generally, R CT can significantly affect the device performance in two ways: (1) lower R CT leads to lower total internal resistance, which is better for the FF; (2) lower R CT also implies effective reduction of I3 − to I− at the interface of electrolyte/CE, which could favor the dye-regeneration process at the interface of electrolyte/photoanode, reflected in the improvement of J SC [46,47]. As shown in Fig. 5, the R CT of the hybrid H-GNs/GF and L-GQDs/GF CE were seven times less than that of the H-GNs CE, therefore the improved J SC and relatively high FF were attributed to the low R CT due to its 3D hybrid nanoarchitecture according to the SEM (Fig. 2) and CV (Fig. 4). Compared with pure GNs CE, the low diffusion impedance of hybrid CE results in the relatively high V OC [3]. These superior electrocatalytic activities of 3D hybrid L-GQDs/GF nanoarchitecture boost its application potential to replace Pt CE in DSSC.

We have demonstrated a novel nanoarchitecture of variable size GNs incorporated into 3D GF network as an efficient hybrid CE for DSSCs. In this 3D hybrid nanoarchitecture with the high conducting GF network as the fast electron transfer channel, the charge injection between the GNs and FTO is easy so that the electron transfer rate of GNs/GF is improved. Especially, this novel hybrid system has been highlighted that the GNs were efficiently isolated from each other to reduce the aggregations and restacking of GNs, which increase the active defective sites for efficiently reducing the I3 −. By comparison with the large-sized H-GNs, the small-sized L-GQDs with an average dimension less than 100nm have more active defective sites at the more edges available for the reduction of I3 − ions. The superior electrocatalytic response of the hybrid L-GQDs/GF CE is ascribed to more active defective sites and a low charge transfer resistance. The DSSC with this 3D hybrid L-GQDs/GF CE shows an enhanced efficiency (η =7.70%), which is extremely close to that (η =7.68%) of Pt. Therefore, interesting findings show that this 3D hybrid L-GQDs/GF nanoarchitecture has a great potential as high efficiency CE for DSSC applications and merits further investigation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been partly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 50972091), 863 project Grants (2013AA031502) and the project of 2011RFLXG006, Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (NCET-10-0066), the Shanghai Municipal Science & Technology Commission key supporting project (No. 12120502900) and industrial research fund from Wuhu Token Sciences Co., Ltd. Prof. Lei Huang appreciates the support of The Program for Professor of Special Appointment (Eastern Scholar) at Shanghai Institutions of Higher Learning.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2014.12.099.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: This document contains supplementary Figs. S1–S5 and Tables S1 and S2.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New method to prepare graphite nanocomposites
